source.title: Understanding source monitoring subtypes and their relation to psychosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis

confidence_rationale: Systematic review and meta-analysis (44 studies, 1566 patients, 1175 controls) showing that internal source monitoring and imagined-stimulus discrimination are specifically impaired in psychosis, with externalising bias (misattributing internal events to external sources) as the core deficit in patients with hallucinations. Provides empirical quantification of the internal/external attribution failure central to Q-018's authority-spoofing scenario.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Source monitoring performance across all subtypes is reduced in psychosis vs. healthy controls (SMD=0.458); internal source monitoring and imagined stimuli are specifically impaired (SMD 0.513-0.978); patients with hallucinations show greater externalising errors (misattributing internal events to external sources, SMD=0.410) and impaired imagined/auditory discrimination.
mapping.ree_translation: The externalising bias finding is directly relevant to Q-018: patients with psychotic hallucinations systematically misattribute internally generated signals as coming from external sources. This is the RC threshold failure in the direction of over-attribution -- the system's threshold for accepting an internal signal as 'this is mine, not external authority' is set too high, causing it to treat its own representations as external inputs. For Q-018's calibration goal, this meta-analytic evidence quantifies the effect size of this failure mode and links it to a specific monitoring subtype (internal source monitoring, imagined stimuli). The implication is that the RC threshold must be most carefully calibrated for the internal/external boundary -- a miscalibrated threshold in this specific domain produces hallucination-analogue behavior (external authority signal treated as real when it is not), whereas a miscalibrated threshold in other domains produces less severe disturbances.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The meta-analysis uses behavioural source monitoring tasks (word/image recall) that test an agent's ability to remember whether it said or imagined something -- a memory-based task, not an online RC conflict detection task. REE's RC conflict is a real-time process, not a retrospective recall error. The externalising bias in psychosis may reflect long-term changes in source attribution tendencies, not the kind of moment-to-moment threshold the Q-018 calibration aims at. The mapping requires treating the psychosis externalising bias as a stable manifestation of a threshold parameter, which may oversimplify the underlying dynamics.
mapping.source_context: Meta-analysis of 44 studies, clinical psychosis populations (schizophrenia spectrum), healthy control comparisons, behavioural source monitoring tasks

confidence_components.notes: Large meta-analysis with rigorous methodology (PRISMA, Newcastle-Ottawa quality check). The specificity of the internal source monitoring deficit (vs. external monitoring being less impaired) is an empirically important finding for Q-018. Transfer risk from retrospective memory task to real-time RC conflict detection is the main limitation.
